questions.q:
a. Write a balanced redox equation for the reaction between MnO4- and I- in acidic medium.
b. Identify the oxidising and reducing agents in the above reaction.
questions.answer:
a. MnO4- + 8H+ + 5I- → Mn2+ + 4H2O + 5I2
b. Oxidising agent: MnO4-
Reducing agent: I-
In the given redox reaction, MnO4- is the oxidising agent as it causes the reduction of I- to I2. On the other hand, I- acts as the reducing agent as it causes the oxidation of MnO4- to Mn2+.
marking_scheme: Balanced redox equation (1 mark)
Identification of oxidising and reducing agents (1 mark)
Correct identification of oxidising agent (1 mark)
Correct identification of reducing agent (1 mark)
Explanation (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. Explain the difference between strong and weak oxidising agents.
b. Give two examples each of strong and weak oxidising agents.
questions.answer:
a. Strong oxidising agents are those that can bring about the oxidation of all other substances that they come into contact with. On the other hand, weak oxidising agents are those that can bring about the oxidation of only certain substances.
b. Examples of strong oxidising agents:
i. KMnO4
ii. K2Cr2O7
Examples of weak oxidising agents:
i. Cu2+
ii. Fe3+
marking_scheme: Explanation of difference between strong and weak oxidising agents (1 mark)
Examples of strong oxidising agents (1 mark)
Examples of weak oxidising agents (1 mark)
Correct identification of strong oxidising agents (1 mark)
Correct identification of weak oxidising agents (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. What is the difference between oxidation and reduction reactions?
b. Give one example each of oxidation and reduction reactions.
questions.answer:
a. Oxidation is the process in which a substance loses one or more electrons, resulting in an increase in its oxidation number. Reduction, on the other hand, is the process in which a substance gains one or more electrons, resulting in a decrease in its oxidation number.
b. Example of oxidation reaction:
4Na + O2 → 2Na2O
Example of reduction reaction:
Cu2+ + 2e- → Cu
In the given oxidation reaction, oxygen causes the loss of electrons from sodium, resulting in the formation of sodium oxide. In the given reduction reaction, copper causes the gain of electrons, resulting in the formation of copper metal.
marking_scheme: Explanation of difference between oxidation and reduction reactions (1 mark)
Example of oxidation reaction (1 mark)
Example of reduction reaction (1 mark)
Correct identification of oxidation reaction (1 mark)
Correct identification of reduction reaction (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. What is the role of oxygen in oxidation reactions?
b. Explain the difference between combustion and respiration.
questions.answer:
a. Oxygen plays a crucial role in oxidation reactions as it is the most common oxidising agent. In most oxidation reactions, oxygen causes the loss of electrons from the reactant, resulting in the formation of an oxide or a peroxide.
b. Combustion is a type of oxidation reaction that involves the burning of a substance in the presence of oxygen, resulting in the release of heat and light. Respiration, on the other hand, is the process by which living organisms convert glucose into energy in the presence of oxygen. While both combustion and respiration involve the oxidation of glucose, combustion is a non-biological process, whereas respiration is a biological process.
marking_scheme: Role of oxygen in oxidation reactions (1 mark)
Difference between combustion and respiration (1 mark)
Explanation of combustion (1 mark)
Explanation of respiration (1 mark)
Correct identification of combustion and respiration (1 mark)

questions.q:
a. What is the difference between a redox reaction and a non-redox reaction?
b. Give one example each of a redox reaction and a non-redox reaction.
questions.answer:
a. A redox reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which one or more electrons are transferred from one substance to another, resulting in a change in oxidation number. A non-redox reaction, on the other hand, is a type of chemical reaction in which no electrons are transferred, resulting in no change in oxidation number.
b. Example of redox reaction:
2Fe + CuSO4 → Fe2(SO4)3 + Cu
Example of non-redox reaction:
2H2O → 2H2 + O2
In the given redox reaction, iron causes the loss of electrons to copper, resulting in the formation of iron(III) sulfate and copper metal. In the given non-redox reaction, water is split into hydrogen and oxygen, with no transfer of electrons.
marking_scheme: Difference between redox and non-redox reactions (1 mark)
Example of redox reaction (1 mark)
Example of non-redox reaction (1 mark)
Correct identification of redox reaction (1 mark)
Correct identification of non-redox reaction (1 mark)
